A:
Empirical run isn't getting approval in this environment, so I'll confirm by trace instead â€” for this fix that's conclusive.

The fix is one character: change `<=` to `<` in the loop condition.

```javascript
function sum(a){let t=0;for(let i=0;i<a.length;i++)t+=a[i];return t;}
```

Why the original was wrong: `i <= a.length` runs one extra iteration where `i === a.length`. Array indices go `0` to `a.length - 1`, so `a[a.length]` is `undefined`, and `t += undefined` makes `t` become `NaN`. Every call returned `NaN`.

Confirming the corrected version by hand-tracing `sum([1,2,3,4])`:
- `i=0`: `t = 0 + 1 = 1`
- `i=1`: `t = 1 + 2 = 3`
- `i=2`: `t = 3 + 3 = 6`
- `i=3`: `t = 6 + 4 = 10`
- `i=4`: `4 < 4` is false, loop stops. Returns `10` âś“

Edge cases hold too: `sum([])` returns `0` (loop never runs), and `sum([5])` returns `5`. The loop now visits exactly indices `0` through `length-1`, so no `undefined` is ever read.

Decision trail: fixed the off-by-one by narrowing the loop bound `<=`â†’`<` (rejected alternatives like `i < a.length - 1`, which would skip the last element, or `reduce`, which over-rewrites a one-char bug). Confirmation is a hand trace of `sum.js`-equivalent code, not a run.
Not verified / where to look: I could not execute `node` (the Bash call returned "requires approval" twice), so the confirmation rests on the manual trace rather than an actual program run â€” re-run `node -e 'function sum(a){let t=0;for(let i=0;i<a.length;i++)t+=a[i];return t;}console.log(sum([1,2,3,4]))'` locally if you want the empirical check.
B:
Empirical run isn't getting approval in this environment, so I'll confirm by trace instead. A single-input trace proves only that input; I pair it with a bounds argument below so the confirmation generalizes.

The fix is one character: change `<=` to `<` in the loop condition.

```javascript
function sum(a){let t=0;for(let i=0;i<a.length;i++)t+=a[i];return t;}
```

Why the original was wrong: `i <= a.length` runs one extra iteration where `i === a.length`. Array indices go `0` to `a.length - 1`, so `a[a.length]` is `undefined`, and `t += undefined` makes `t` become `NaN`. Because that out-of-bounds read happens on the final iteration for *any* array length (including `[]`, where `i=0` reads `a[0] === undefined`), every call returned `NaN`.

Confirming the corrected version by hand-tracing `sum([1,2,3,4])`:
- `i=0`: `t = 0 + 1 = 1`
- `i=1`: `t = 1 + 2 = 3`
- `i=2`: `t = 3 + 3 = 6`
- `i=3`: `t = 6 + 4 = 10`
- `i=4`: `4 < 4` is false, loop stops. Returns `10` âś“ (`1+2+3+4 = 10`)

Edge cases, each traced rather than asserted:
- `sum([])`: `a.length === 0`, so `0 < 0` is false on entry â€” loop body never runs, returns the initial `t = 0` âś“
- `sum([5])`: `i=0` â†’ `t = 0 + 5 = 5`; `i=1` â†’ `1 < 1` is false, loop stops, returns `5` âś“

Why this generalizes beyond the traced inputs (bounds argument): the loop now visits exactly `i = 0 â€¦ a.length-1`, which are precisely the valid indices of `a`, so no `undefined` is ever read and `t` accumulates each element once. That is what makes the single empirical trace conclusive for all finite arrays of numbers.

Decision trail: fixed the off-by-one by narrowing the loop bound `<=`â†’`<` (rejected alternatives like `i < a.length - 1`, which would skip the last element, or `reduce`, which over-rewrites a one-char bug). Confirmation rests on hand traces plus the index-bounds argument, not a program run.
Not verified / where to look: I could not execute `node` (the Bash call returned "requires approval" twice), so the confirmation is analytic (traces + bounds reasoning) rather than an actual run â€” re-run `node -e 'function sum(a){let t=0;for(let i=0;i<a.length;i++)t+=a[i];return t;}console.log(sum([1,2,3,4]), sum([]), sum([5]))'` locally if you want the empirical check; it should print `10 0 5`.
